fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that compression/archive toolkit we've been talking about. The core idea is that users should be able to take raw bytes, run them through various compression formats, and get them back out intact — all in memory, no file system touching. We also need to handle the reading/writing side of things for binary data (think header parsing, integer decoding, that kind of thing). Checksumming needs to work both as a one-shot call and as a streaming/chunked approach, similar to how we handled the incremental digest stuff in the old hash utility module.

For the archive side, we need ZIP and TAR support. ZIP should handle password-protected files (same crypto approach the old zip utility used), non-ASCII filenames, and the big-file extension. TAR needs to cover the various historical flavors and long filename extensions. Both need round-trip guarantees.

One thing that came up in review: the collection management piece needs to handle duplicate names gracefully — ask Sarah about the exact behavior we agreed on for ordering. Also the error handling story needs to be clean; external callers can't be seeing internal exceptions bubble up.

Compression-wise we need DEFLATE, zlib, gzip, bzip2, and xz. The whole thing should be organized properly — no giant single files please, we got burned on that with the old codec monolith. I/O contract goes through JSON stdin/stdout for the adapter layer.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: def handle(op: str, params: dict) -> str

trigger_keywords: crc32 output format
how should checksum be displayed
hex digits for crc
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: CRC-32 values must be printed as exactly 8 lowercase hexadecimal digits, zero-padded. For example, a value of 0 is printed as 00000000. The one-shot op prints 'crc32=<value>' and the streaming digest op prints 'digest=<value>' using the same 8-digit format.

trigger_keywords: crc32 of empty input
checksum with no data
empty byte sequence crc
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The CRC-32 of an empty byte sequence is zero, reported as crc32=00000000. The Adler-32 of an empty sequence is the initial seed value 1, reported as adler32=00000001.

trigger_keywords: repeat parameter behavior
feed data multiple times
accumulate checksum across repetitions
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The optional 'repeat' integer feeds the same data sequence that many times, accumulating the running checksum across repetitions. The result equals the checksum of the data concatenated repeat times end-to-end. This applies to both CRC-32 and Adler-32 one-shot and streaming ops.

trigger_keywords: streaming digest finalization
digest byte order
big endian checksum bytes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The finalized digest for both crc32_digest and adler32_digest is the four big-endian bytes of the 32-bit checksum, printed as 'digest=<value>' in 8-digit lowercase hex. This is numerically identical to the one-shot value but the output key is 'digest' not 'crc32' or 'adler32'.

trigger_keywords: byte input format
how to pass bytes in json
base64 input encoding
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Byte sequences may be given in three interchangeable forms: a JSON array of integers 0-255; an object {"b64": "<base64>"} for standard base64-encoded bytes; or an object {"pattern": "mod256", "length": N, "mod": M} where the i-th byte is i % M (M defaults to 256). All ops that accept byte data must handle all three forms.

trigger_keywords: cursor peek behavior
look ahead without advancing
non-destructive read
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'peek' command in the read op looks ahead N bytes without advancing the cursor, emitting 'peek=<hex>'. The 'skip' command advances N bytes emitting nothing. The 'reset' command rewinds to position 0 emitting nothing. The 'eos' command emits 'eos=true' or 'eos=false' without advancing.

trigger_keywords: nul terminated string reading
fixed length string vs null terminated
string command behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The bare 'string' command reads a NUL-terminated text run, consuming the NUL terminator, and emits 'str=<text>' verbatim (without the NUL). The {"string": N} form reads exactly N bytes as text and emits 'str=<text>'. In both cases the text is printed verbatim, not as hex.

trigger_keywords: windowed sub-view behavior
subset cursor independence
non-copying slice
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For op read_subset: read one byte from the base cursor, take sub-view sub1 of 3 bytes at the current position, take sub2 starting at offset 1 within sub1 (length 2). Reading sub1 emits sub1=<hh> lines for each byte, then eos=true/false for sub1. Reading sub2 emits sub2=<hh> lines. Each view's cursor is independent.

trigger_keywords: little endian integer writing
growable output buffer
write_bytes_ramp pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: write_bytes_ramp writes 'count' bytes where byte i has value i % mod, appending to a growable buffer. write_ints writes 'count' integers each of 'width' bytes in little-endian order, where the i-th integer value is base+i. Both ops report 'length=<total bytes>' and 'crc32=<8hex>' of the buffer.

trigger_keywords: deflate streaming decode
inflate chunk size
streamed inflate behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: inflate_stream compresses the input data at the given level, then feeds the compressed stream in fixed 'chunk'-sized pieces through a streaming decompressor, concatenating decoded blocks. The final recovered bytes must match the original. Output is 'length=<n>' and 'crc32=<8hex>' of the recovered bytes.

trigger_keywords: zip password decryption
zipcrypto support
encrypted zip entry
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: ZIP decoding must support ZipCrypto password-protected entries. The 'password' field in the input provides the decryption key as a string. After decryption, the entry's CRC-32 must be verified. Output follows the same format as unencrypted zip_decode: files count and per-entry name, size, isfile flag, and crc32.

trigger_keywords: zip64 extension handling
large zip file support
zip non-ascii filenames
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: zip_decode must handle zip64 size extensions for large entries and non-ASCII (UTF-8) entry names. The 'comment' field from the end-of-central-directory record must be printed as 'comment=<text>' before the file count when present. Directory entries appear in the file list but isfile=false.

trigger_keywords: tar dialect support
gnu long name extension
tar header fields output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: tar_decode must parse old GNU, star, and classic V7 dialects. For each entry output: files count (once), then name, mode (decimal), uid, gid, size, modtime (Unix timestamp decimal), type (single char, '0' for regular file), uname, gname, link. GNU long-name (@LongLink) entries must reconstruct the full filename for the following entry.

trigger_keywords: duplicate name replacement behavior
archive collection ordering
replace existing entry
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In archive_build, adding an entry whose name already exists replaces the existing entry's content in place without changing the order or count. The collection preserves insertion order. Output: 'length=<count>' then per entry 'entry=<name> b0=<hex first byte>'. If 'clear' is true, all entries are removed after processing and length=0 with no entry lines.

trigger_keywords: invalid archive error format
malformed input error handling
error output line format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When an operation cannot complete due to invalid input (e.g. bytes that are not a valid archive), the adapter prints exactly one line: 'error=invalid_archive\n'. No stack trace, exception class name, or additional lines may appear. This applies to tar_invalid and any parse failure in tar_decode or zip_decode.

trigger_keywords: xz empty stream
xz checksum verification
xz decode output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
xz_decode must handle streams with no integrity check and streams with CRC-32, CRC-64, or SHA-256 checks. An empty xz stream decodes to zero bytes, output as 'length=0
crc32=00000000
'. All xz decode outputs follow 'length=<n>
crc32=<8hex>
'.

trigger_keywords: tar symlink type flag
symbolic link entry parsing
link field in tar
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Symbolic link entries in tar have type flag '2' and the 'link' field contains the target path. The output for a symlink entry includes 'type=2' and 'link=<target>'. Regular file entries have type '0' and link is empty string.

trigger_keywords: read_int output format
integer width padding
both byte orders
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: read_int decodes the first 'width' bytes (2, 3, 4, or 8) of data under both byte orders and prints 'le=<hex>\nbe=<hex>\n'. Each value is padded to exactly 2*width hex digits. Width 2 gives 4 digits, width 8 gives 16 digits.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'tar_decode', 'tar_roundtrip', 'tar_invalid', 'zip_roundtrip', 'zip_decode', 'archive_build', 'crc32_value', 'crc32_digest', 'adler32_value', 'adler32_digest', 'read', 'read_int', 'read_subset', 'write_bytes_ramp', 'write_ints', 'deflate_roundtrip', 'inflate_stream', 'zlib_decode', 'zlib_roundtrip', 'gzip_roundtrip', 'gzip_decode', 'bzip2_roundtrip', 'bzip2_decode', 'xz_decode'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how we handled the incremental digest stuff in the old hash utility module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The streaming checksum interface: a stateful object fed data in chunks and then finalized, mirroring both crc32_digest and adler32_digest ops as described in features 1.2 and 2.2 of start.md

context_pointers.vague_reference: same crypto approach the old zip utility used
context_pointers.actual_meaning: ZipCrypto (traditional PKZIP encryption) decryption as required by feature 11.3 zip_password test case, using the 'password' field from the JSON input

context_pointers.vague_reference: ask Sarah about the exact behavior we agreed on for ordering
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Duplicate-name replacement preserves insertion order and count: feature 12.1 archive_build test where adding entry 'b' a second time replaces content in-place without reordering or increasing length

context_pointers.vague_reference: the various historical flavors and long filename extensions
context_pointers.actual_meaning: TAR dialect support: old GNU, star, and classic V7 headers (feature 10.1) plus GNU @LongLink long-name extension entries (feature 10.2) as described in start.md feature 10

context_pointers.vague_reference: no giant single files please, we got burned on that with the old codec monolith
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Architecture constraint NFR-1: the codebase must be organized as a multi-file directory tree (e.g. src/, lib/, tests/) with strict separation of checksums, byte I/O, compression codecs, and container formats into distinct modules

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify directly — you'll want to check the PRD (start.md) and the public test case JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the authoritative specification. If you've already read those and still have a question, try looking at the specific expected_output strings in the relevant feature test file, as they encode the exact format contract.

feature10_1_tar_headers.json.description: Parse a tar archive and report, for every entry in order, its header metadata: the entry name, the numeric permission mode, owner and group numeric ids, the byte size, the modification time as a Unix timestamp, the single-character type flag, the owner and group names, and the linked-file name. Several historical tar dialects (old GNU, star, classic V7) must all be parsed into the same normalized header fields.

feature10_4_tar_roundtrip.json.description: Build an archive in memory from named entries with byte content, encode it to the tar format, then decode the encoded bytes back into entries. The decoded entry names and content must match what was put in. The output reports the entry count and each decoded entry's name and content in hexadecimal.

feature10_5_tar_invalid.json.description: Attempt to parse bytes that are not a valid tar archive. Parsing must fail rather than producing a bogus result; the failure is reported as a neutral error category line.

feature11_1_zip_roundtrip.json.description: Build an archive in memory from named entries with byte content, encode it to the zip format, then decode the encoded bytes back with checksum verification. Entries may opt out of compression (stored). Content of any byte shape is preserved, and non-ASCII entry names round-trip correctly. The output reports the decoded entry count and, per entry, the name, the uncompressed size, and the CRC-32 of the content.

feature11_2_zip_decode.json.description: Parse a zip archive produced by an external tool and report the archive comment when present, the number of entries, and for each entry the name, the uncompressed size, whether it is a file (as opposed to a directory entry), and the CRC-32 of its decompressed content. Archives using stored and deflated entries, directory entries, non-ASCII names, and the zip64 size extension must all be parsed.

cases.expected_output:
comment=This is a zipfile comment.
files=2
name=test.txt size=26 isfile=true crc32=c3edd7c0
name=gophercolor16x16.png size=785 isfile=true crc32=54d531fe

feature11_3_zip_password.json.description: Parse a password-protected (ZipCrypto) zip archive given the correct password, decrypting and verifying the single contained entry. The output reports the entry count and the entry's name, size, file flag, and content CRC-32.

feature12_1_replace_file.json.description: Maintain an ordered collection of named archive entries. Adding an entry whose name already exists replaces the existing entry's content in place without changing the order or count. The output reports the entry count and, in order, each entry's name and first content byte.

cases.expected_output:
length=3
entry=a b0=00
entry=b b0=03
entry=c b0=02

feature12_2_clear.json.description: Clearing the collection removes all entries. The output reports the resulting entry count and lists no entries.

feature1_1_crc32_value.json.description: Compute the CRC-32 checksum of a sequence of bytes and report it as a fixed eight-digit lowercase hexadecimal value. An optional repeat count feeds the same byte sequence that many times, accumulating the running checksum across repetitions so a large input can be expressed compactly.

feature1_2_crc32_stream.json.description: Compute a CRC-32 checksum incrementally by feeding one or more chunks of bytes through a streaming checksum object, then finalize it. The finalized digest is the four big-endian bytes of the 32-bit checksum, reported as a fixed eight-digit lowercase hexadecimal value. An optional repeat count adds the same chunk that many times before finalizing.

feature2_1_adler32_value.json.description: Compute the Adler-32 checksum of a sequence of bytes and report it as a fixed eight-digit lowercase hexadecimal value. The checksum of an empty input is the initial seed value. An optional repeat count feeds the same byte sequence that many times, accumulating the running checksum.

feature2_2_adler32_stream.json.description: Compute an Adler-32 checksum incrementally by feeding one or more chunks of bytes through a streaming checksum object, then finalize it. The finalized digest is the four big-endian bytes of the 32-bit checksum, reported as a fixed eight-digit lowercase hexadecimal value. An optional repeat count adds the same chunk that many times before finalizing.

feature3_1_byte_reader.json.description: Read from an in-memory byte buffer using a positioned cursor that advances as data is consumed. A program of read commands is executed in order against one buffer. Commands include: report the number of bytes remaining; read a single byte; look ahead at the next N bytes without advancing the cursor; advance the cursor by N bytes without reading; read a block of N bytes; read a NUL-terminated text run; read a fixed number of bytes as text; rewind the cursor to the start; and report whether the cursor is at end of stream. Each command that yields a value emits one labelled line; single bytes and byte blocks are reported in lowercase hexadecimal, text is reported verbatim.

feature3_2_fixed_integers.json.description: Decode a fixed-width unsigned integer from the front of a byte buffer under both little-endian and big-endian byte orders, given the width in bytes (2, 3, 4, or 8). The same buffer is interpreted under each byte order and the two decoded values are reported as hexadecimal, each padded to the integer's full byte width.

feature3_3_subset_views.json.description: Create independent windowed views over a shared byte buffer without copying. After reading one byte from the base buffer, a sub-view of a given length is taken at the current position, and a further sub-view is taken at an offset within that sub-view. Reading through each view advances only that view's own cursor; the bytes yielded by each view and the end-of-stream state of the first view are reported.

cases.expected_output:
sub1=bb
sub1=cc
sub1=dd
eos=true
sub2=cc
sub2=dd

feature4_1_byte_writer.json.description: Append bytes one at a time to a growable in-memory output buffer, then report the total number of bytes written and the CRC-32 checksum of the resulting buffer as eight-digit hexadecimal. The input specifies how many bytes to write; each byte's value is its index taken modulo a given base.

feature4_2_integer_writer.json.description: Append fixed-width unsigned integers in little-endian byte order to a growable output buffer, then report the total byte length and the CRC-32 checksum of the buffer as eight-digit hexadecimal. The input specifies the integer width in bytes, how many integers to write, and a base value; the i-th integer written is the base plus i.

feature5_1_deflate_roundtrip.json.description: Compress a byte buffer with the DEFLATE algorithm at a given compression level, then decompress the result. The recovered bytes must equal the original input regardless of level. The output reports the length and CRC-32 of the recovered bytes.

feature5_2_streamed_inflate.json.description: Decompress DEFLATE data incrementally: the compressed stream is fed in fixed-size chunks and decoded blocks are pulled out as they become available, then concatenated. The recovered bytes must equal the original uncompressed input. The output reports the length and CRC-32 of the recovered bytes. The input gives the original data, the compression level used to produce the stream, and the streaming chunk size.

feature5_3_zlib_block_decode.json.description: Decode a single zlib-wrapped (DEFLATE inside a zlib header/checksum envelope) block produced by an external tool, recovering the original bytes. The output reports the length and CRC-32 of the decoded bytes.

feature6_zlib_roundtrip.json.description: Compress a byte buffer into the zlib container format, then decompress it with checksum verification. The recovered bytes must equal the original. The output reports the length and CRC-32 of the recovered bytes.

feature7_1_gzip_roundtrip.json.description: Compress a byte buffer into the gzip container format, then decompress it with checksum verification. The recovered bytes must equal the original. The output reports the length and CRC-32 of the recovered bytes.

feature7_2_gzip_decode.json.description: Decompress a gzip stream produced by an external tool, validating the embedded CRC-32 and length trailer. The output reports the length and CRC-32 of the decoded bytes.

feature8_1_bzip2_roundtrip.json.description: Compress a byte buffer with the bzip2 algorithm, then decompress it with checksum verification. The recovered bytes must equal the original. The output reports the length and CRC-32 of the recovered bytes.

feature8_2_bzip2_decode.json.description: Decompress a bzip2 stream produced by an external tool, verifying the per-block checksums. The output reports the length and CRC-32 of the decoded bytes.

feature9_xz_decode.json.description: Decompress an xz stream produced by an external tool. The xz container may carry no integrity check or one of several check types (CRC-32, CRC-64, or SHA-256); when checksum verification is requested the embedded check must validate. An empty xz stream decodes to no bytes. The output reports the length and CRC-32 of the decoded bytes.
